SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
![Do Well Do Good](https://dowelldogood.com/wp-content/themes/dwdg-theme/resources/img/logo-dwdg.svg)

[Home](https://dowelldogood.com/) » The untapped potential of 3D-printed prostheses for BoP populations

# The untapped potential of 3D-printed prostheses for BoP populations

![](https://dowelldogood.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/09/prostetic-1273185.jpeg)

**400 million people globally need a prosthesis, but over 85% do not have access to one. Hence, there is potential for businesses to develop good quality but affordable solutions in this segment**

According to the WHO, 0.5% of humans need a prosthesis, or 400 million people globally,  and only 5 to 15% of amputees in any given population have access to a prosthesis, i.e. an artificial limb. Regions with the most trauma-related amputations are East Asia and South Asia, followed by Western Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East, then high-income North America and Eastern Europe. In Africa alone, estimates bring the number of amputees around 6.5 millions, and prostheses are a luxury for most of them.

While the demand is already considerably higher than the offer, the **amputee population is expected to double by 2050, doubling an already underserved market.**

As per the World Health Organisation (WHO), **only 1 in 10 persons located in low-resource areas has access to adequate prosthetic technology**, due to barriers of pricing and infrastructure availability.

Given the size and expected growth of the market, there are potentially many **opportunities to set up businesses** and create value that benefits society. Given the high number of amputees not being able to afford regular prostheses that fit them, there is a need for affordable yet comfortable and practical prosthesis. 3D printing has demonstrated its capacity to produce such devices and cater to underserved populations, in remote and low-infrastructure areas.

**3D printing of limbs solves the problem of cost, quality & access to infrastructure**

There are multiple examples through which 3D printing in prosthetics is helping overcome the price barrier.

3D printing is also reinforcing access, as poorly-fitted traditional limbs often discourage amputees to use a prosthesis. Since the printer adjusts to the data file it is given, the original file only needs to be altered with the results of the patient’s scanner to create custom-tailored prosthesis adapted to the users’ residual limb and the specific tasks they perform in daily life. It also allows for a more user-centric approach, fostering more emotional engagement, which is speculated to develop more ownership of the limb, particularly for children.

3D-printed prostheses help solve accessibility for people in remote areas or those without access to medical infrastructures. Instead of creating a mold and coming back 4 or 5 times to adjust it, then baking it and adding the resin, a simple compact scanner can be used. This little device replaces heavy equipment in a dedicated center and the method also requires less professionals. Scanner results are sent to a modelization software and printed. A professional then assesses the fit and gives instruction via video call, removing the barrier of a remote or underserved area. An important challenge, as per the World Health Organisation (WHO), **only 1 in 10 persons located in low-resource areas has access to adequate prosthetic technology**.

**While around half of the 3D prosthesis makers are charities, 4 archetypes of business models exist among current players in 3D printing for prosthetic limbs**

Half of the 3D prostheses players are charities, while the other half is made of (often small – 10-70 employees) businesses. It is worth noting that while NGOs are present globally, businesses are mostly represented in developed countries, although some examples can be found outside of them, like Instalib in the Philippines or Cure Bionics in Tunisia.

**To scale this further, we need firms and institutions encourage entrepreneurship**

While the technology exists, the 3D-printed prosthetic market for BoP is still largely untapped. To remedy this situation, both the private sector and governments have a role to play.

**The private sector** would be well-inspired to get more contact with universities and learn about their (often cheap due to restricted funding) innovations, which can open them the market of BoP populations. Point Design is a successful example, as it commercializes upper limb prosthetics developed by two Professors of the Biomechatronics Development Laboratory at the University of Colorado.

**Public institutions** also have their role to play to encourage the development of these businesses. Incentives have already proved their usefulness, as the example of Touch Bionics shows. Founded in 2005 and now part of Ossur, it is a spin-out of NHS Lothian and InnoScot Health, a public body to foster innovation in healthcare. It was sold in 2016 to Ossur, a listed global leader in orthopedics with 4,000 employees in 30 countries.

For example, a South African university student invented a 3D printed bionic prosthesis in 2014, which was awarded prizes in prestigious innovation competitions. The creator tried to turn Touch Hand into a company, was able to enter an accelerator and benefited from a free entrepreneurship training workshop and a grant to the university by the Technology Innovation Agency. However, despite these dispositives, which are not coordinated, almost 10 years later, the project remains within the university and relies on sponsors for its development and is still not offered to customers.,,,

Hence, innovation and technology are not the only necessary elements to boost the penetration of 3D printing in healthcare or other domains. Entrepreneurship training and support structures, subsidies or funds for businesses demonstrating their inclusivity are also needed.

![Do Well Do Good](https://dowelldogood.com/wp-content/themes/dwdg-theme/resources/img/logo-dwdg.svg)

We love working with ambitious people. Let's build something great together now.

###### Prosthetics

# [3D Printed Prosthetics: Affordable Limb Solutions Transforming Lives](https://choiceprosthetics.com/3d-printed-prosthetics-affordable-limb-solutions/)

In recent years, the field of prosthetics has witnessed a remarkable transformation, driven by advancements in technology and materials science. Among the most significant innovations is the rise of 3D printed prosthetics, which are revolutionizing the way we think about limb replacement and rehabilitation. These cutting-edge devices not only offer a more affordable alternative to traditional prosthetic limbs but also provide customized solutions tailored to individual needs. As a result, these prosthetics are not just enhancing mobility; they are transforming lives by empowering individuals with greater independence and improved quality of life.

In this article, we will explore the various aspects of **[3D printed prosthetics](https://choiceprosthetics.com/prosthetics/)**, highlighting how these affordable limb solutions are making a profound impact on those who rely on them. From the technology behind the printing process to real-life success stories, join us as we delve into this exciting intersection of innovation and human resilience.

## 1. Impact of 3D Printed Prosthetics on Accessibility & Affordability

The advent of 3D printed prosthetics has revolutionized the accessibility and affordability of limb solutions, particularly for individuals in underserved communities. Traditional prosthetic limbs are often prohibitively expensive, making them inaccessible to many who need them. However, 3D printing technology has significantly reduced the cost of manufacturing prosthetics, making them more affordable and accessible to a wider population.

This has had a profound impact on individuals who previously could not afford traditional prosthetics, providing them with a newfound sense of mobility and independence. Furthermore, 3D printed prosthetics have also addressed the issue of accessibility in remote or low-resource areas. In regions where access to medical facilities and prosthetic services is limited, this technology has enabled the local production of customized prosthetics.

This localized approach not only reduces the logistical challenges associated with traditional prosthetic procurement but also empowers communities to address the specific needs of their members. As a result, individuals in these areas can now receive tailored **[prosthetic solutions](https://choiceprosthetics.com/prosthetics-and-orthotics-improve-life-for-amputees/)** without the barriers of distance and cost.

## 2. How 3D Printed Prosthetics are Making Limb Solutions More Accessible

### i. Cost-Effective Production

The cost-effectiveness of 3D printing is attributed to its digital design and additive manufacturing techniques, which allow for precise and efficient production of prosthetic components. This reduction in costs has made it possible for organizations and individuals to provide prosthetic solutions to those who were previously unable to afford them.

### ii. Customization and Personalization

The customization capabilities of 3D printed prosthetics have further enhanced accessibility. Each prosthetic limb can be tailored to fit the unique anatomical and functional requirements of the individual, ensuring optimal comfort and functionality. This personalized approach not only improves the overall user experience but also addresses the diverse needs of individuals with limb differences.

### iii. A Game-Changer in Accessibility

As a result, this advance technology has become a game-changer in making limb solutions more accessible to a broader spectrum of individuals, regardless of their financial means or geographical location. This democratization of prosthetic limbs has the potential to improve the lives of millions of people worldwide.

![Choice P&O Advanced Technology](https://choiceprosthetics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Advanced-Technology-2-683x1024.jpg)

![Choice P&O Advanced Technology](https://choiceprosthetics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Advanced-Technology-2-683x1024.jpg)

## 3. The Impact of Affordable Prosthetics on People’s Lives

The availability of affordable prosthetics through 3D printing has had a profound impact on the lives of individuals with limb differences. For many, access to a prosthetic limb can mean the difference between dependence and independence. The ability to perform daily tasks, engage in physical activities, and participate in social interactions is significantly enhanced with the use of a well-fitted and functional prosthetic device.

Additionally, affordable prosthetics empower individuals to pursue educational and employment opportunities that may have been previously out of reach. In addition to the physical benefits, affordable prosthetics also contribute to the psychological well-being of their users. The restoration of mobility and the ability to blend into social settings without drawing attention to their limb differences can boost self-esteem and confidence.

This positive impact on mental health is invaluable and underscores the transformative effect that affordable prosthetics can have on people’s lives. Ultimately, the availability of affordable prosthetic solutions through 3d printing not only restores physical capabilities but also fosters a sense of inclusion and empowerment for individuals with limb differences.

## 4. Advancements in 3D Printing Technology for Prosthetic Limbs

The field of 3D printing technology for prosthetic limbs has witnessed significant advancements in recent years, leading to improved functionality, durability, and aesthetics of prosthetic devices. One notable development is the use of advanced materials such as carbon fiber-reinforced thermoplastics and flexible polymers, which offer enhanced strength and lightweight properties. These materials not only contribute to the durability of the prosthetic limbs but also ensure greater comfort and natural movement for the users.

Besides, advancements in 3D scanning and modeling technologies have facilitated the creation of highly accurate and customized prosthetic designs. By capturing precise anatomical data and translating it into digital models, prosthetists can create tailored solutions that closely mimic the natural form and function of the missing limb. This level of customization has significantly improved the overall fit and performance of 3D printed prosthetics, enhancing the user experience and satisfaction.

Ongoing research in biofabrication techniques holds promise for the development of **[biocompatible materials](https://xometry.pro/en/articles/3d-printing-biocompatible/)** that can integrate with the body’s tissues, potentially leading to more seamless interfaces between the residual limb and the prosthetic device. These advancements in 3D printing technology continue to push the boundaries of what is possible in the field of prosthetic limb solutions, offering new opportunities for individuals with limb differences to lead fulfilling and active lives.

## 5. The Future of 3D Printed Prosthetics: Innovations and Challenges

As 3D printed prosthetics continue to evolve, several innovations are poised to shape the future landscape of limb solutions. One area of focus is the integration of electronic components and sensory feedback systems into prosthetic devices, allowing users to experience a greater sense of control and natural movement. Advancements in robotics and artificial intelligence are also driving the development of smart prosthetics that can adapt to various activities and environments, further enhancing their functionality.

The concept of decentralized manufacturing using 3D printing holds potential for expanding access to prosthetic solutions globally. By establishing local production hubs equipped with this technology, communities can rapidly respond to the specific needs of individuals requiring prosthetic care. This decentralized approach not only reduces lead times for obtaining prosthetic devices but also fosters local expertise in design and fabrication, promoting sustainability and self-sufficiency.

However, alongside these innovations, challenges such as regulatory standards, reimbursement policies, and long-term durability of 3D printed prosthetics remain areas of consideration. Ensuring the safety and efficacy of these devices, as well as addressing issues related to insurance coverage and maintenance, will be crucial for widespread adoption and acceptance. In conclusion, this advance technology has emerged as a transformative force in making limb solutions more accessible and affordable for individuals with limb differences.

## Conclusion: Empowering Lives with 3D Printed Prosthetics

At Choice Orthotics & Prosthetics, we are proud to be at the forefront of this evolution. As the **[premier prosthetics center in Knoxville](https://choiceprosthetics.com/prosthetics/)** and East Tennessee, our experienced clinicians are dedicated to utilizing the latest technologies, including 3D printed prosthetics, to help our patients achieve their vitality goals. We understand that each individual’s journey is unique, and we strive to provide personalized solutions that enhance mobility and improve quality of life.

If you or a loved one is considering a prosthetic solution or simply want to learn more about how 3D printing can benefit your specific needs, we invite you to reach out to us. Contact **Choice Orthotics & Prosthetics** today to schedule a free consultation. Together, we can explore the possibilities and take meaningful steps toward achieving your goals for an active and fulfilling life.

# 3D-Printed Prostheses — Accessible and Affordable

## A new process for manufacturing prosthetic limbs could be a game-changer for amputees around the world.

Ed Brown

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Nine out of 10 amputees in the world don't have access to a proper prosthetic. The startup company, LIMBER Prosthetics & Orthotics, Inc. aims to do something to address this problem by 3D printing complete one-piece structurally sound prosthetic limbs.

LIMBER started as a grassroots, student-driven research effort at University of California, San Diego, taught by structural engineering Professor Falko Kuester.

###### Related Articles

[Smart Artificial Limbs](/component/content/article/tb/pub/briefs/bio-medical/28163)

[Dynamic Prosthetic Socket Sensor Detects Limb Changes for Best Fit](/component/content/article/tb/tv/30998)

They were trying to improve the process for building a custom prosthetic. The traditional method requires prosthetists who are like sculptors. They carve a model of the residual limb by hand — a time-consuming process that is expensive and can’t be easily scaled with current mass manufacturing techniques.

That’s when Kuester approached one of his Ph.D. students, Luca De Vivo Nicoloso, with the idea of printing prosthetics.

A year later, De Vivo, who was then a teaching assistant in the class, Joshua Pelz, who was then a Ph.D. student in the class and Herb Barrack, a certified prosthetist and orthotist with more than three decades of experience, decided to form the company.

“To begin, we scan the remaining leg with the facial recognition camera on the iPhone (model 10 or later). With that scan, we construct a model of the limb,” said Pelz. But, added De Vivo, “It’s not only the scan that gets used to design the missing limb — we use that as a starting point for the geometry. A certified prosthetist on our team takes manual dimensions to supplement the scan, and only then designs the prosthetic leg. The prosthetics need to be personalized to account for individual issues. So, it needs to be designed by a person with knowledge about what a prosthetic limb should look like for that specific person.”

“We start when an amputee goes into a prosthetic clinic, or, in the case of developing countries, a rehabilitation or medical center. Preference is always that a prosthetist collects 3D scan and manual measurements (often possible even in underserved/developing countries). If this is not possible, LIMBER can train someone with anatomical/medical knowledge to collect the data we need,” said Pelz.

According to the team, the data is sent to the LIMBER cloud, where a series of design technicians and certified prosthetists retrieve it and design the personalized limb. The design is then sent to their 3D printing facilities, where their technicians print it, post-process it, package it, and ship it to the prosthetic clinic or medical center where the patient and the prosthetist are located.

“The last stage of the work is when the prosthetist or medical specialist fits the leg. Although good starting data will allow us to get close, there may be cases where dynamic alignment is necessary. If so, thermoforming can be used with our technology to make adjustments,” added Pelz.

This is a process that, in the U.S., can be done in one to two days from the moment the patient gets scanned, to the delivery. We have also done it across borders from San Diego to Ukraine in less than seven days. This is something that can be world-reaching,” said De Vivo.

**AT A GLANCE**

**INNOVATORS:** Herb Barrack, Cofounder; Luca De Vivo Nicoloso, Cofounder; Joshua Pelz, Cofounder.

**INNOVATION:** A new process for the 3D printing of complete one-piece structurally sound prosthetic limbs.

**IMPACT:** These prosthetics are affordable and accessible and could be a game-changer for amputees around the world.

LIMBER’s business plan is two-fold. The company plans to sell its personalized prostheses in developed countries while providing its services for developing countries at discounted prices or for free. “We feel the best way to accomplish that, is working with local institutions — people on the ground who know the community, the culture, the needs of their population. For example, we are working in Mexico with community members in a local Rotary Club,” said Pelz.

To date, they’ve printed legs custom designed to fit 15 different people — and did not have to re-print any of them. Four required slight alignment adjustments and two required slight socket fit adjustments, which were easily made by a prosthetist using a heat gun with a specially designed applicator.

The goal for LIMBER is to be selling 3D-printed prosthetic limbs in the U.S. in the first quarter of 2024.

*This article was written by Ed Brown, Associate Editor, SAE Media Group. For more information, visit [here](https://www.limberprosthetics.com) .*

FULL CONTENT:
# [Enabling The Future](https://enablingthefuture.org/)

A Global Network Of Passionate Volunteers Using 3D Printing To Give The World A "Helping Hand."

## e-NABLE is Global | Powerful | Helping | Growing

Thanks to thousands of volunteers around the world, e-NABLE is making a big difference in people’s lives

Thanks to thousands of volunteers around the world, e-NABLE is making a big difference in people’s lives

[![](https://i0.wp.com/enablingthefuture.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/e-nable_hub_banner.jpg?resize=800%2C133)](https://hub.e-nable.org/)

![](https://i0.wp.com/enablingthefuture.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/e-nable_hub_banner.jpg?resize=800%2C133)

## What is e-NABLE?

e-NABLE is an online global community of “Digital Humanitarian” volunteers from all over the world who are using their 3D printers to make free and low-cost prosthetic upper limb devices for children and adults in need. The open-source designs created by e-NABLE Volunteers help those who were born missing their fingers and hands or who have lost them due to war, natural disaster, illness or accidents.

There are approximately 40,000 e-NABLE volunteers in over 100 countries who have delivered free hands and arms to an estimated 10,000-15,000 recipients through collaboration and open-source design to help those in underserved communities who have little to no access to medical care. Our volunteers are working hard to “Give the World a Helping Hand” and would love to have you join us!

![](https://i0.wp.com/enablingthefuture.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/11/FINALneedhand-1.png?fit=200%2C200&ssl=1)

##### Welcome to e-NABLE!

## Latest Stories

![](https://i0.wp.com/enablingthefuture.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/09/e-nable.jpeg?resize=300%2C150&ssl=1)

## [e-NABLE is Still Going Strong 10 Years Later](https://enablingthefuture.org/2022/09/27/e-nable-is-still-going-strong-10-years-later/)

It’s hard to believe that it has been nearly 10 years since the original metal prototype for the first 3D printed e-NABLE device was created for one child in need …

![St. Joseph’s Academy, an all-girls Catholic High School in St. Louis, MO showing 3D printed e-NABLE devices they created](https://i0.wp.com/enablingthefuture.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/image-4.png?resize=300%2C150&ssl=1)

FULL CONTENT:
![University of Nebraska Omaha logo](//www.unomaha.edu/_files/images/uno-icon-50.png)
![University of Nebraska Omaha logo](//www.unomaha.edu/_files/images/uno-icon-50-noline.png)

# Cyborg Beast

## Related Content

### Additional Resources

![a neon green 3d printed prosthetic hand called the cyborg beast.](images/cyborg-beast-header-2.jpg)

![a neon green 3d printed prosthetic hand called the cyborg beast.](images/cyborg-beast-header-2.jpg)

## Our Mission

Our mission is to create customized low-cost prosthetic and orthotic solutions for underserved populations, and provide technical support to educational and medical institutions desiring to offer low cost devices using 3D printing technology.

Our long term goal is to develop a line of visually appealing, easy to assemble, low-cost 3D printed prosthetic and orthotic devices for children with upper and lower limb differences. The aim is to facilitate the promotion of normal growth and development of motor skills by encouraging active participation in sports and other recreational activities.

## The Cyborg Beast

Our low-cost, 3D-printed prosthetic hand named "Cyborg Beast" was designed using modeling software called Blender and manufactured in the researcher's laboratory using desktop 3D printers.

Elastic cords placed inside the dorsal aspect of the fingers provide passive finger extension. Finger flexion is driven by non-elastic cords along the palmar surface of each finger and is activated through 20-30 degrees of wrist flexion.

The result is a composite fist (flexing the fingers towards the palm) for gross grasp.

### Materials

The materials used for printing our prosthetic hand are polylactide (PLA) plastic and acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS).

Other components of the prosthetic hand include Chicago screws of various sizes, 1 mm lift nylon cord, 1.5 mm elastic cord, Velcro, medical-grade firm padded foam, protective skin sock, and a dial tensioner system.

### Cost

The majority of these materials are available at local hardware stores or online, costing approximately $50 USD. The weight of a fully assembled hand at 140% of its original size is 184.2 grams. A similar device costs approximately $4,000 and weighs about 400 grams.

### Assembly Time

The average time to fully assemble the prosthetic hand design is approximately 2.5 hours.

### Use

The Cyborg Beast prosthetic hand is well suited for activities that involved the manipulation of light objects using lateral, power (composite), and spherical prehensile patterns.

enriched_points.expanded_content: 3D printing significantly reduces production costs, making prosthetics accessible for low-income individuals, transforming lives by enhancing mobility without overwhelming financial burdens.

Localized production capabilities allow communities to create custom prosthetics, ensuring individuals in remote areas receive tailored solutions, overcoming logistical barriers and enhancing accessibility.
With a projected doubling of amputations by 2050, 3D-printed prosthetics can address global needs, fostering independence and inclusion across diverse socio-economic landscapes.
